failure_surface: Destination rows 8-10 show only partial data (column I='Credit Note') while all other columns contain None/null values, indicating incomplete row population despite loop claiming 6 rows copied.
root_cause: The agent incorrectly determined where new data should start in the destination sheet. The start_row calculation checked if the last row had data but defaulted to row 5 anyway, overwriting existing data in rows 5-7. Combined with broad try/except blocks that silently swallowed errors during value assignment or formatting operations, partial rows were left incomplete without any error feedback. The termination condition also didn't prevent creating partially-filled rows beyond the expected range.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before writing new rows, properly scan the destination worksheet to find the last non-empty row using actual data detection, not assumptions about max_row. Remove or narrow try/except blocks so value assignment failures surface as exceptions instead of silently producing incomplete rows. Verify each row is fully populated before incrementing the copy counter.
evidence: Verification output shows rows 8-10 have 'Credit Note' in column I but all other selected columns (A-G, L-M) are None
Code uses 'start_row = dest_ws.max_row + 1 if dest_ws.cell(row=dest_ws.max_row, column=1).value else 5' which defaults to 5 regardless of actual data presence
Multiple bare except/pass blocks in column formatting operations (lines catching font/fill/border alignment) which could mask assignment failures
Output states 'Rows copied: 6' but verification shows only rows 5-7 fully populated while 8-10 are incomplete
Destination sheet already had data in rows 3-6 before modification attempt

memory_items.title: Validate Destination Data Boundaries Before Writing
memory_items.lesson: Do not rely on max_row alone to determine where to append data; scan actual cell values to find the true last populated row in the destination worksheet.
memory_items.generalization: When appending to an existing worksheet, iterate backward from max_row or forward from known header row to detect the last row with actual content across any column, not just column A.

memory_items.title: Avoid Silent Failure in Cell Operations
memory_items.lesson: Broad try/except blocks that pass without logging can hide critical failures during value or formula assignment, resulting in partially-written rows.
memory_items.generalization: Wrap cell modifications in try/except but log or re-raise exceptions to ensure write failures are visible; do not silently continue with incomplete rows.

memory_items.title: Verify Complete Row Writes During Batch Operations
memory_items.lesson: After completing a batch row copy operation, verify that each affected row contains the expected number of populated cells before finalizing.
memory_items.generalization: Implement post-write validation that counts non-null cells per row against expected column count, flagging incomplete rows for retry or manual review.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Append Data Safely to Existing Worksheets
- Do not assume `max_row` indicates the last data row; scan multiple columns to find the true end of content.
- Check for empty rows before starting to insert new data to avoid overwriting existing entries.
- Use explicit row detection logic: iterate from header row downward until finding consecutive empty rows.

### Handle Exceptions Explicitly in Cell Operations
- Avoid broad `try/except/pass` patterns around cell assignments; they can silently produce incomplete data.
- Log exceptions or re-raise them when value/formula/cell property assignments fail.
- After batch operations, verify each modified row has the expected number of populated cells.

### Validate Post-Write State
- Run a sanity check after writing: count non-null cells in target rows against expected column count.
- If partial rows are detected, investigate the cause before considering the task complete.
- Consider writing to a temporary file first and validating before overwriting the original workbook.
